title: A Mexican American Childhood in Orange County
summary: Luis Zapata describes growing up in Orange County, California, in a household with a Mexican American father who worked as a mechanical engineer and an Anglo mother who ran a yarn shop. He recalls his parents as liberal Democrats in a politically conservative area.

title: Racial Identity Left Unspoken at Home
summary: Zapata explains that racial and cultural identity were never discussed in his family, leaving him to navigate it alone. He recounts his older brother being barred from dating a young woman because of his ethnicity in the 1950s.

title: Leaving Home Young and Learning Trades
summary: Having left home around age nine or ten, Zapata worked gardens, a paper route, and scraped boat hulls near Newport Beach, diving for coins thrown by tour boats. He became a dishwasher, busboy, short-order cook, and eventually a chef who could find work anywhere.

title: Becoming Political at San Jose State
summary: Zapata recounts entering San Jose State College as a physics major and finding he could not relate to fellow physics students who ignored social issues. Amid the era of Berkeley activism and the Dominican Republic intervention, he grew increasingly political.

title: Farm Worker Organizing and the Call to Mississippi
summary: While organizing alongside Cesar Chavez's farm workers near San Jose, Zapata heard that the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union needed volunteers and raised his hand. He drops out of college and notes that classmate Luis Valdez was among the many politically active Latinos in the area.

title: From Latino Identity to Native American Focus
summary: Zapata reflects that there was no space to explore Latino identity in Mississippi, and that after his time there he focused on Native American issues, arguing that brownness derives primarily from Indigenous heritage. He returns to the practical work of organizing farm workers in the fields.

title: How Field Strikes Were Organized
summary: Zapata describes infiltrating fields in ragged clothes because known organizers were barred by farm thugs, then convincing nearby workers to walk out on strike. He distinguishes the publicity-driven grape boycott from the strawberry and asparagus strikes he actually worked.

title: Rural Isolation and the SNCC Information Network
summary: Zapata reflects on how isolated rural Southerners were, with local papers covering bass-fishing contests rather than national or world news. He notes that SNCC channels often delivered more news of events in Africa and New York than the local media did, and contrasts SNCC's militancy with SCLC.

title: Driving Donated Cars Into Mississippi
summary: Zapata recalls first meeting the head of the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and bringing down a donated car loaded with clothes for the movement. Over the years he drove three cars south, including a hearse they passed off as an ambulance so people would ride in it.

title: A Militant Instinct and Learning by Doing
summary: Zapata traces his belief in fighting back to childhood and describes how movement work, like ironworking and cooking before it, was learned on the run with little explanation. He notes SNCC expected a seasoned union organizer and he figured it out as he went.

title: Strong Women, Ministers, and SNCC's Grassroots Ethos
summary: Zapata names the movement's local backbone as strong women and ministers, the latter being harder for the white establishment to fire. He contrasts SNCC's bottom-up, grassroots decision-making with the top-down style of other civil rights organizations.

title: The One-Room Shack in Cleveland
summary: Zapata describes the one-room shack behind a house in Cleveland that served as the SNCC, COFO, and Mississippi Freedom Labor Union office. Workers slept on the floor behind stacked cinder blocks for protection against drive-by shootings.

title: The Plantation Family Who Hosted Voter Registration
summary: Zapata recounts staying with a tall, well-respected Black landowner who farmed with his family and drove Zapata to clusters of houses for voter registration before sunup. The family fed him enormous breakfasts, and Zapata believes the location was in Washington or Sunflower County.

title: Amzie Moore, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Susie Ruffin
summary: Zapata remembers Amzie Moore as quietly strong and a strategist rather than a church speaker, and recalls Fannie Lou Hamer as a tobacco-chewing force who commanded every room. He pairs Hamer with a close ally he identifies as Susie Ruffin.

title: Organizers: Donaldson, Carmichael, and Moses
summary: Zapata praises Ivanhoe Donaldson's instinct for one-to-one organizing and notes Stokely Carmichael's fearlessness as an organizer. He casts Bob Moses as the intellectual whose thinking shaped SNCC's leadership and its turn toward pan-Africanism.

part: The Summer Project and Freedom Democrats
title: The Summer Project and an Unintended Legacy
summary: Zapata recalls the 1964 Summer Project already being planned when he arrived, while he attended meetings as a newcomer outside the grand strategy. He argues that the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge helped trigger the white exodus into a solidly Republican South.

part: The Summer Project and Freedom Democrats
title: Volunteers, Head Start, and Registering for the MFDP
summary: Zapata describes summer volunteers as green but useful in Head Start schools and clothing-distribution programs, while noting that one-on-one field organizing took more time than a summer allowed. He recalls meetings increasingly focused on signing people up for the MFDP.

part: The Summer Project and Freedom Democrats
title: Based in Cleveland and Organizing in Bars
summary: Zapata recounts keeping his few belongings in Cleveland while sleeping in ditches at places like Delta Pine when being hunted. Unlike most SNCC workers, he organized young people in juke joints over beer, and drove vehicles to precinct meetings rather than taking a front-line role.

part: The Summer Project and Freedom Democrats
title: Driving the Challenge Delegation to Washington
summary: Zapata explains he did not attend the Jackson convention or Atlantic City, but drove a delegation to Washington for the congressional challenge, lodging in All Souls Church basement and lobbying daily. He shares a story of a Washington officer baffled by his carload of bandana-wearing field workers with Mississippi tags.

part: The Summer Project and Freedom Democrats
title: Witnesses and Facilitators, Not Decision Makers
summary: Zapata stresses that his job was logistics and turnout rather than strategy, and that the MFDP relied on local people for leadership. He recalls Lawrence Guyot as a powerful figure and stays neutral on the post-challenge split over endorsing Johnson and Humphrey.

title: Union Strategy and the Limits of Leverage
summary: Zapata describes having more say in union work, including starting a cooperative and weighing whether strikes could hold against plantation owners bringing in replacement labor. He notes that mechanization had reduced leverage, though hand labor like chopping and picking cotton still gave organizers some power.

title: Small Strikes and Limited Wage Victories
summary: Zapata recounts striking individual plantations, setting up food banks, and raising money up north to sustain striking families. The union never coordinated a Delta-wide strike, but won wage increases on several plantations that pressured neighboring owners to match them.

title: The Tent City and a Strike He Missed
summary: Zapata recalls hearing of a strike that led to evictions and a tent city, which he places outside Bolivar County and associates with a local woman organizer named Blackwell. He explains the encampment was set up while he was up north and that it had internal problems.

title: Fundraising Trips and the SNCC Newsletter
summary: Zapata explains SNCC scheduled his fundraising trips to California, New York, Boston, and Washington, where local SNCC offices collected checks, food, and clothing. He recalls staff staying connected through WATS-line phone calls and a New York newsletter he attributes to a SNCC artist whose name he is unsure of.

title: The Union Director and the Waveland Tensions
summary: Zapata describes the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union director, whom he names as Lee Bankhead and identifies as a woman from the Cleveland area, as thoughtful and well organized. He recalls the resentment toward white volunteers who tried to make decisions, a tension discussed at the Waveland meeting he did not attend.

title: An Office Colleague and Freedom Songs
summary: Zapata mentions a dear friend, a SNCC office worker he describes as tremendously organized and analytical and likens to Bob Moses, though he says she rarely came into the field. He turns to the role of music and his favorite movement stories.

title: Deputy Weeks and a Firebombed Porch
summary: Zapata tells of a brutal deputy he names as Weeks who once put a shotgun in his chest, and whose attitude changed sharply after someone threw a firebomb on his porch. He also recalls wiring SNCC cars so the taillights could be switched off when being chased at night.

title: The Deacons for Defense Visit Cleveland
summary: Zapata recounts a small group from the Deacons for Defense and Justice coming up from Louisiana to meet local people, holding several meetings where he lived even though SNCC was officially nonviolent. He notes the standing debate over whether nonviolence was a moral commitment or a tactic.

title: Weighing Self-Defense Against Firepower
summary: Zapata recalls telling local people that taking up their hunting rifles against the armed power structure would not work. He returns to the isolated case of Deputy Weeks, the on-camera shotgun incident, and the proliferation of out-of-control auxiliary police in Mississippi.

title: The Meredith March and News That Did Not Reach Him
summary: Asked about the 1966 Meredith March where Carmichael raised the phrase black power in Greenwood, Zapata says he does not remember it because national news rarely reached the Delta. He describes SNCC turning the march into a voter-registration campaign after the Voting Rights Act.

title: Mass Arrest at the Jackson Fairgrounds
summary: Zapata recalls the large Jackson demonstration, likely in 1965, where everyone was arrested and held at the fairgrounds, men and women in separate pens. He started in solitary confinement as a suspected organizer, and recounts witnessing a jailer assault a woman who fought back.

title: Shielding People and the Worst Jail
summary: Zapata describes lying over people to shield them from clubs during demonstrations, and seeing an arrest record inches thick. He recalls the worst jail, a single sloped Black cell with an overflowing toilet and urine-soaked mattresses, and carving chess pieces from melted foam cups to pass time.

part: Latinos, Communication, and the Union Documents
title: Adapting to Years in Mississippi
summary: Reflecting on four years in Mississippi, Zapata says he believed in changing the system and accepted his role as a minion doing work he believed in. He recalls returning to California to share information with farm workers between stretches in the Delta.

part: Latinos, Communication, and the Union Documents
title: Latinos in SNCC and the Segmented Farm Strikes
summary: Zapata notes how few Latinos were in SNCC and that a researcher has since connected him with others, though he is unsure of their backgrounds. He describes California farm-worker organizing as segmented by crop, with separate strawberry, tomato, and grape strikes in the Delano and San Joaquin valleys.

part: Latinos, Communication, and the Union Documents
title: Isolation After Leaving and the Union Buttons
summary: Zapata reflects that once you left a place you were isolated, making it hard to learn what happened in Mississippi afterward. Shown old Mississippi Freedom Labor Union documents he believes he typed, he mentions keeping a SNCC pin and a Freedom Labor Union button depicting a hand holding a lantern.

part: Latinos, Communication, and the Union Documents
title: Typing Flyers on a Portable Typewriter
summary: Zapata describes typing union materials on a little portable typewriter and on church typewriters, with meetings run by the union director and attended by ministers, Susie Ruffin, and sometimes Fannie Lou Hamer. He explains copies were made by mimeograph and a stencil-cutting machine rather than photocopiers.

part: Latinos, Communication, and the Union Documents
title: Mimeograph, the Atlanta Print Shop, and Sign-Ups
summary: Zapata contrasts their hand-cranked mimeograph work with the print shop SNCC eventually ran in Atlanta, and recalls asking northern visitors to bring printed flyers. He describes signing people up at the back of churches after speeches and freedom singing, often filling in forms for those who could not read.

part: Good Times, Delta Pine, and Leaving
title: Hardship, Joy, and Freedom Songs
summary: Zapata insists that alongside beatings there were good times: dancing, laughter, intimate relationships, and songs sung in jail, fields, and churches. He sings a verse of We Are Soldiers in the Army, recalling he sang bass and that the Freedom Singers would tear everyone up.

part: Good Times, Delta Pine, and Leaving
title: Delta Pine and Memories of the Communist Party
summary: Zapata recalls voter-registration work at Delta Pine, an old-style plantation with sharecropper cabins, where elderly people by the river remembered Communist Party organizers floating in by boat decades earlier. He realized the 1960s were not the beginning of organizing in Mississippi.

part: Good Times, Delta Pine, and Leaving
title: Hiding Out and the Run to Yazoo City
summary: Zapata recounts hiding in ditches for days while trucks searched for them at Delta Pine, eating uncooked greens, his companion forced to shave his matted hair. He recalls being met by police in Yazoo City and ordered to turn around or face months in jail, and his inability to recall the companion's name, a nephew of Fannie Lou Hamer.

part: Good Times, Delta Pine, and Leaving
title: Deciding It Was Time to Leave
summary: Zapata explains he left Mississippi when many new groups and volunteers arrived and he no longer felt essential. While doing fundraising in Washington he was recruited by a cooks, hotel, and restaurant workers union that paid a real salary during a citywide strike.

title: Union Work, Mexico, and Native Religion
summary: Zapata recounts staying in Washington for union work, then traveling extensively through Mexico, Central and South America, where he engaged deeply with traditional Native religion and became a priest able to perform marriages. He worked foundries in Minneapolis and ranches in Arizona while doing political work.

title: What the Movement Taught Him About Fear
summary: Zapata reflects that the movement taught him to organize across differences and broadened his horizons. He describes never having a good sense of fear, a trait that carried through farm-worker beatings and ironworking on towers and bridges, leaving him a bulldog for what he believes is right.

title: Returning for the Mike Espy Campaign
summary: Zapata describes taking a van of volunteers back to the Delta for Mike Espy's congressional campaign and seeing how much the towns had changed while the rural areas had not. He recalls Espy winning by 82 votes and using that margin to show every volunteer that their work mattered.

title: The MFDP Button and a Political Quilt
summary: Zapata shows an official Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party button he believes is among the few left, alongside his collection of SNCC and CORE buttons. He describes making his daughter a quilt stitched from decades of political-event T-shirts, a record of his activist history.

part: Reflections on Leadership and Later Activism
title: Fannie Lou Hamer's Command of the Room
summary: Zapata returns to Fannie Lou Hamer, recalling that her real power was dominating meetings where major SNCC figures deferred to her rather than her public speeches. He remembers debates over running symbolic candidates and over the literacy tests, from interpreting the Constitution to guessing beans in a jar.

part: Reflections on Leadership and Later Activism
title: A Hostile FBI and the Programs People Built
summary: Zapata recalls that registering voters remained hard even after the Voting Rights Act, with a Southern-staffed FBI that mostly upheld the system. He describes well-funded groups like the Delta Ministry arriving, and the all-hands ethos of building Head Start programs and fixing up buildings around the clock.

part: Reflections on Leadership and Later Activism
title: Grenada, Failed Causes, and Moral Victories
summary: Zapata describes designing ferrocement fishing boats for revolutionary Grenada's bottom-up government before the United States invasion ended the project. He recounts a fondness for difficult causes, including two Jesse Jackson campaigns and a losing race where he served as a coordinator against far better-funded opponents.

part: Reflections on Leadership and Later Activism
title: The Zapatistas and Indigenous Land in Chiapas
summary: Zapata explains working with the Zapatistas in Chiapas, where mostly Mayan and other Indigenous groups reclaimed land held by absentee owners and faced hired paramilitary police. He recalls going down to document several massacres of men, women, and children.

part: Reflections on Leadership and Later Activism
title: Electoral Power as a Tool for Justice
summary: Zapata describes a support group pressuring federal authorities in Mexico, with a phone call from a senator's office helping halt police mayhem. He argues that elected officials respond to the ability to turn out votes, crediting that grunt work for marriage equality and the Dream Act passing in Maryland.

title: The Voting Rights Decision and a Wider Lens
summary: Zapata names the 2013 Supreme Court decision gutting part of the Voting Rights Act as a present concern, noting courts are hard to influence through political work. He reflects that his focus widened beyond the United States from the isolation of the South to struggles worldwide, including the Palestinian cause.

title: La Raza and the Letter-Writing Era
summary: Zapata says he was one of the original members of La Raza, a distant member based on the East Coast, alongside a colleague he describes as a close kinswoman. He reflects on how organizing once depended on letters, and that he still has boxes of political correspondence in his attic.

title: The DC Statehood Party and Local Lefties
summary: Zapata recalls working in the DC Statehood Party after arriving in Washington, alongside local movement people. He names key figures including Julius Hobson, Josephine Butler of a US-Soviet friendship society, and Hilda Mason.

title: Immigration, Borders, and Persistent Racism
summary: Zapata argues that borders are artificial and questions who invited the pilgrims, framing immigration through the displacement of Indigenous people. He warns that despite political attention to Latino issues, struggling schools and persistent racism show the Voting Rights Act addressed only a small part of poverty and inequality.

title: A Missing Generation of Activists
summary: Zapata observes that some young people are deeply involved while a middle generation largely was not, and faults activists of his era for not raising their own children to be active. He notes that today's youth are far better informed about politics and the wider world than he was at twenty-one.

title: Fighting It One Step at a Time
summary: Zapata closes by warning of sophisticated corporate efforts to suppress change and urging that the struggle be fought one step at a time. He explains he works one-on-one rather than in classrooms, telling people it is not what it was like but what it is like, as the interview ends.
